summary: DHH traces his history with AMD from dismissing them as budget parts in the 1990s to now running AMD in every machine he owns. After 37signals switched from Intel to AMD EPYC servers in the early 2020s, DHH went all-in on AMD for desktops, laptops, and even his kids' gaming PCs after switching to Linux. He argues that TSMC's manufacturing is the key enabler lifting AMD and Qualcomm to near-parity with Apple's M-series chips, while Intel falls further behind by insisting on its own fabrication. The post celebrates AMD's decades-long journey from perpetual underdog to legitimate competitor.
key_points: 37signals switched entirely from Intel to AMD EPYC servers, running 96-core machines that outperform Intel on price/performance
TSMC is the hidden protagonist — its advanced manufacturing nodes are what enabled Apple's M-series lead and are now closing that gap for AMD and Qualcomm
Intel is falling behind because it insists on its own chip fabrication rather than leveraging TSMC's superior processes
Qualcomm's Snapdragon 8 Elite now matches or beats Apple's A18 Pro, ending years of Android CPU inferiority
Framework laptops paired with AMD chips represent a credible, repairable alternative to MacBooks for Linux developers
money_quotes: Today, the bulk of our new fleet run on dual EPYC 9454s for a total of 96 cores(!) per machine. They're awesome.
TSMC really is lifting all the boats behind its innovation locks.
It was so bad for a while that we had to program our web apps differently for Android, because they simply didn't have the power to run JavaScript fast enough! But that's all history now.
After dumping Apple, it's a delight to see that the M-series advantage is shrinking to irrelevance fast.
By focusing on repairability, upgradeability, and superior keyboards, we finally have an alternative for developer laptops that doesn't just feel like a cheap copy of a MacBook.

The company was founded just one year after Intel, back in 1969, but for its entire existence, it's lived in the shadow of its older brother. Now, thanks to TSMC, great leadership from Lisa Su, and a crack team of chip designers, they're finally reaping the rewards.
There's of course still the software reason for why someone would pick Apple, and they continue to make solid hardware, but the CPU playing field is now being leveled.
key_insight: TSMC's manufacturing dominance is democratizing chip performance, enabling AMD and Qualcomm to close Apple's lead while Intel falls further behind by going it alone.
